band_5_example: We have enough problems on Earth, so why should we spend billions of dollars to build a dome on a dusty red planet where nobody can breathe?
band_9_fix: Mars colonization is 'species-redundancy'; it serves as a 'planetary-back-up' against an extinction-level catastrophe on Earth, ensuring that the human 'logic-experiment' continues even if our primary habitat fails.

keyword: ielts speaking part 3: deep sea exploration and mining risks
problem_name: The ocean is deep and dark and there is nothing there
band_5_example: I think the deep sea is just water and sand, and it's better to focus on the surface where we have fish and oil and boats for trade.
band_9_fix: The deep-sea is the 'final-biological-frontier'; unregulated mining for 'battery-minerals' risks destroying specialized ecosystems and carbon-sinks that we don't yet fully understand, potentially triggering a climate-feedback loop.

keyword: ielts writing task 2: biodiversity credit markets: placing a value on nature
problem_name: We should save the trees because they are beautiful to look at
band_5_example: The reason we shouldn't cut down the forest is because it's sad when the animals die and the view from the window is not nice anymore.
band_9_fix: Biodiversity-credits attempt to 'internalize-the-externality'; by placing a measurable fiscal value on intact ecosystems, we create the economic-incentive necessary for corporations to prioritize preservation over exploitation.

keyword: ielts task 2: soil degradation and global food security
problem_name: Farmers just need to use more fertilizer to grow more food
band_5_example: If the plants aren't growing well, you just add more chemicals to the dirt and then everything will be fine and everyone will have bread.
band_9_fix: Modern chemical-intensive agriculture is 'eroding-the-foundation'; it masks soil-degradation with short-term yields, leading towards a structural-collapse of arable land that no amount of fertilizer can eventually fix.

keyword: ielts speaking part 1: talking about using public transport: efficiency
problem_name: The bus is good because it's cheap and better for the sky
band_5_example: I like taking the bus or the train because it saves me money and it's better for the environment than driving my own private car.
band_9_fix: High-density transit is a 'mathematical-necessity'; it is the only way to move population-masses through urban-conduits without creating the 'logistical-stagnation' that private vehicles inevitably produce.

keyword: ielts writing task 2: urban heat island effect and city design
problem_name: Cities are hot because the sun is shining more now
band_5_example: The reason it's so hot in the city is because of global warming, and we just need more air conditioning in every room to stay cool.
band_9_fix: The urban-heat-island is a 'structural-trapping' of thermal energy; concrete and asphalt reflect heat back into the city, necessitating 'passive-cooling-architecture' like green-roofs and white-pavements to lower the temperature.

keyword: ielts task 2: fossil fuel subsidies and market distortion
problem_name: Gasoline should stay cheap so that poor people can drive
band_5_example: The government should give money to oil companies to keep the price of petrol low, because otherwise people can't go to work or buy food.
band_9_fix: Fossil-fuel subsidies are a 'market-contamination'; they artificially depress the price of carbon, making renewable alternatives look more expensive than they actually are and delaying the transition to a sustainable energy-grid.

keyword: ielts speaking part 2: describe a time you used the internet to solve a problem
problem_name: I searched for a recipe and cooked a delicious meal for my family
band_5_example: I wanted to cook something special, so I went to YouTube and found a video, and then I followed the steps and everything was very tasty.
band_9_fix: I utilized a 'specialized-repository' to bridge my 'knowledge-deficit'; in the digital era, the bottleneck is not informational access, but the ability to curate and synthesize the correct data for a specific goal.

keyword: ielts writing task 2: e-waste recycling and the circular economy
problem_name: We should just put old TVs and phones in the trash bin
band_5_example: When I get a new phone, I just throw the old one in the garbage because it's broken and the battery doesn't work and it's useless now.
band_9_fix: E-waste is a 'toxic-goldmine'; it contains rare minerals that are destructive to extract but valuable to recover. A 'bottled-resource' model would require manufacturers to take responsibility for recovering these materials.

keyword: ielts task 2: planned obsolescence ethics in consumer electronics
problem_name: Phones break fast because the parts are cheap and bad
band_5_example: Companies can't make good phones anymore because they want to save money, so the items break after a few years and you have to buy another.
band_9_fix: Planned obsolescence is a 'deliberate-structural-expiration'; it is engineered to maximize 'recursive-revenue' by ensuring that the product fails just as the next model is launched, regardless of the physical durability of the components.

keyword: ielts speaking part 3: the benefits of a minimalist lifestyle
problem_name: Buying more things makes you happy and successful
band_5_example: I think you should work hard so you can buy a big house and a fast car and many clothes, because that's how people know you are a winner.
band_9_fix: Minimalism is 'cognitive-unloading'; by reducing the volume of physical possessions, you eliminate the 'decision-friction' and maintenance-burden they impose, freeing up your bandwidth for high-status, creative output.

keyword: ielts writing task 2: the impact of the 4-day work week on productivity
problem_name: People will work less and the business will lose money
band_5_example: If workers only go to the office for four days instead of five, they will be lazy and they will get 20% less work done for the company every week.
band_9_fix: The 4-day work-week leverages 'marginal-utility'; by eliminating the 'fatigue-plateau' of the fifth day, workers can maintain a higher 'logic-intensity' during their active hours, often matching or exceeding their previous output.

keyword: ielts task 2: mental health in corporate culture: the burnout crisis
problem_name: Working hard and being stressed is just a part of a good job
band_5_example: If you want to make a lot of money, you have to be ready to work 12 hours a day and be stressed and tired all the time because that is life.
band_9_fix: Burnout is a 'systemic-overload'; when corporate protocols prioritize short-term targets over long-term 'psychological-sustainability', the resulting turnover and productivity-loss create a massive hidden-cost for the organization.

keyword: ielts speaking part 3: the role of the public domain in AI training
problem_name: AI should be allowed to read everything and learn from it
band_5_example: Computers need data to be smart, so they should be allowed to use all the books and photos online without asking anyone or paying any money.
band_9_fix: The 'AI-training-commons' is an 'intellectual-property-minefield'; we must balance the 'innovation-premium' of open-data with the need to protect individual creators from having their specialized-output harvested without consent.

keyword: ielts writing task 2: universal secondary education vs vocational focus
problem_name: Everyone needs to finish high school to be a smart citizen
band_5_example: The government should make every child study history and literature until they are 18, so they have a broad mind before they start working.
band_9_fix: Mandatory general education often leads to 'academic-inflation'; for many, a pivot to 'skills-based-vocational-apprenticeships' at age 16 provides a more direct and high-status path to economic independence and specialized expertise.

keyword: ielts task 2: the glass ceiling and corporate boardrooms
problem_name: Women don't reach the top because they choose to stay with kids
band_5_example: The reason there are fewer female CEOs is because women like to take care of their families and they don't want the stress of leading a big company.
band_9_fix: The glass-ceiling is a 'structural-invisible-barrier'; it is maintained by 'legacy-selection-bias' and 'non-inclusiveworkplace-protocols' that penalize caregivers while rewarding those who can demonstrate a 24/7 availability.

keyword: ielts speaking part 3: meat consumption and environmental impact
problem_name: Cows are bad because they make too much gas for the sky
band_5_example: I heard that eating beef is bad for global warming because of the methane and the water that the animals need to drink every single day.
band_9_fix: Livestock production represents a 'caloric-inefficiency'; we use massive amounts of plant-protein to produce a small amount of animal-protein, effectively wasting 90% of the potential energy in the trans-species conversion.

keyword: ielts writing task 2: carbon offsetting skepticism vs true reduction
problem_name: Planting trees lets you fly without feeling guilty about it
band_5_example: If I pay a few dollars to plant a tree, then I can take a plane to Europe and it's like I never produced any carbon at all because the tree will fix it.
band_9_fix: Carbon-offsetting is often a 'moral-shortcut'; it allows corporations to maintain their 'emissions-as-usual' protocols. True sustainability requires 'at-source-decarbonization'—fixing the mechanism of production rather than buying a clean-conscience.

keyword: ielts task 2: nuclear energy vs renewables in base-load power
problem_name: Nuclear is too scary to use for the whole country's electricity
band_5_example: We should only use wind and solar because nuclear power plants can explode and then everything is dangerous for a thousand years like Chernobyl.
band_9_fix: Nuclear power provides the only 'high-density-baseload'; it is the essential 'energy-anchor' that allows a grid to survive cloudy or calm days without reverting back to fossil-fuels for stability.

keyword: ielts speaking part 1: talking about using e-scooters in the city
problem_name: E-scooters are fun but they are dangerous and messy on the street
band_5_example: I like riding e-scooters because they are fast and I don't have to walk, but I think people just leave them everywhere and they look very messy.
band_9_fix: Micro-mobility solutions are 'first-and-last-mile-bridgers'; they eliminate the 'logistical-gap' between a transit-hub and a final-destination, effectively reducing the need for short, inefficient private vehicle trips.

keyword: ielts writing task 2: remote learning outcomes vs classroom socialization
problem_name: Kids learn more when they are sitting in a room with a teacher
band_5_example: If you are in a classroom, you have to pay attention and you have your friends around you, so you learn faster and you are more happy at school.
band_9_fix: Physical classrooms provide 'social-calibration'; the unstructured interaction between peers is as important as the curriculum, as it develops the 'emotional-intelligence' that digital modules cannot yet simulate.

keyword: ielts task 2: screen time and adolescent brain development: dopamine loops
problem_name: Apps are just for fun and they don't hurt anyone's brain
band_5_example: TikTok and Instagram are just games and videos, and if kids play too much they might be a bit tired but their brains will be exactly the same as before.
band_9_fix: Short-form video content exploits 'dopamine-asymmetry'; it provides constant, high-frequency rewards that can 'rewire' the adolescent attention-span, making the sustained deep-focus needed for complex-logic increasingly difficult to achieve.

keyword: ielts speaking part 3: influencer marketing ethics and the youth
problem_name: Influencers are just people who show us cool things on their phone
band_5_example: I follow influencers because they have a great life and they tell me which clothes and phones are the best to buy so I can be like them.
band_9_fix: Influencer marketing utilizes 'parasocial-logic'; it masks a commercial transaction as a peer-recommendation, bypassing the critical-defenses that an audience would normally use when viewing a traditional advertisement.

keyword: ielts writing task 2: digital nomad taxes and local infrastructure
problem_name: Nomads should work for free because they are visitors
band_5_example: If someone comes to my country with their laptop and works for a company in America, they shouldn't have to pay any tax here because they are just on holiday.
band_9_fix: Digital-nomads utilize 'municipal-infrastructural-leverage' without contributing to the fiscal-base; a 'remote-work-visa' model is necessary to ensure they pay for the services and legal-security they utilize while in a host nation.

keyword: ielts task 2: meat consumption ethics: the factory farm reality
problem_name: Factory farms are good because they make food cheap for everyone
band_5_example: We need large meat factories because otherwise the price would be too high and normal families couldn't afford to have a burger or a steak for dinner.
band_9_fix: Factory-farming is a 'biological-exploitation-model'; it prioritizes 'caloric-output-per-square-foot' over every other variable, creating a systemic-risk for zoonotic diseases and an ethical-deficit that a civilized society must eventually address.

keyword: ielts speaking part 3: the 4-day work week and work-life balance
problem_name: Having three days off every week is the best way to be happy
band_5_example: If I can have a long weekend every single week, I will have more time for my hobby and my family and I will be much more excited to go to work on Monday.
band_9_fix: A shorter work-week can reduce 'logistical-attrition'; with an extra day for life-admin and recovery, workers return to their roles with 'renewed-cognitive-bandwidth', potentially increasing their problem-solving efficiency.

keyword: ielts writing task 2: the impact of the gig economy on social security
problem_name: Uber drivers are happy because they choose their own life
band_5_example: I think it's fair that gig workers don't have pensions, because they are free and they don't have a boss who tells them what to do every morning in the office.
band_9_fix: The gig-economy erodes the 'social-contract'; by classifying workers as 'independent-contractors', platforms effectively externalize the cost of healthcare and retirement, leaving the state or the individual to pay for the long-term structural-deficit.

keyword: ielts task 2: urban farming: decentralized food production
problem_name: Crops should stay on farms in the country where they belong
band_5_example: Cities are for buildings and roads, and we should just bring the food in on big trucks from the farms because they have more space and better dirt.
band_9_fix: Decentralized urban agriculture reduces 'transit-friction'; it turns 'sterile-urban-surfaces' into productive agents, providing a local caloric-buffer that is immune to trans-continental logistical collapses.

keyword: ielts speaking part 3: the geopolitics of rare earth metals: tech sovereignty
problem_name: Minerals for batteries are found in every country in the world
band_5_example: I think we have enough metal for cars and phones everywhere, and we just need to dig a hole and take it out and build the battery easily in a factory.
band_9_fix: The green-transition is bottlenecked by 'mineral-concentration'; a handful of nations control the processing-infrastructure for lithium and cobalt, creating a new form of 'energy-dependence' that is just as strategically volatile as oil was in the 20th century.

keyword: ielts writing task 2: facial recognition technology and systemic bias
problem_name: Cameras never lie so they are always fair to everyone
band_5_example: AI can see everyone's face exactly as it is, so if it finds a criminal, it is 100% correct and it is much better than a human police officer who might make a mistake.
band_9_fix: Facial-recognition suffers from 'algorithmic-opaque-bias'; if the training-data is skewed, the system will disproportionately misidentify certain demographics, potentially automating and accelerating existing systemic prejudices under the guise of 'objective-code'.

keyword: ielts task 2: quantum computing hype vs physical reality
problem_name: Quantum computers will make everyone's lives better next year
band_5_example: In 2026, everyone will have a quantum laptop that can do everything instantly and we will never have to wait for a website to load ever again in our life.
band_9_fix: Quantum computing is currently in the 'noisy-intermediate-scale-era'; while the theoretical potential is massive, the physical-constraints of error-correction and sub-zero-cooling mean that its impact will remain concentrated in high-level research for the next decade.

keyword: ielts speaking part 3: Mars colonization: planetary backup rationale
problem_name: We should fix the Earth and forget about other planets for now
band_5_example: Why are we spending money to build a city on Mars when we have pollution and poverty and wars on our own planet right now? We should fix home first.
band_9_fix: Mars colonization is a 'risk-diversification-strategy'; it is not about 'escaping' Earth, but about ensuring that the sum-total of human knowledge and specialized-intelligence has a 'failsafe-habitat' in the event of an atmospheric or geological catastrophe.

keyword: ielts writing task 2: deep sea mining vs carbon sinks
problem_name: Mining the ocean will give us enough metal for every electric car
band_5_example: The bottom of the sea is full of rocks with metal in them, so we should go there and take them all so we can make as many batteries as we want and save the planet.
band_9_fix: The deep-sea is our 'largest-carbon-reservoir'; unregulated mining risks releasing eons of stored carbon and destroying 'benthic-ecosystems' that provide the foundation for the entire marine food-web.

keyword: ielts task 2: biodiversity credit markets: monetizing the ecosystem
problem_name: You can't put a price on a forest because it is worth a trillion dollars
band_5_example: Nature is for everyone and it is a gift from God, so we shouldn't try to use money and math to say how much a tree or a bird is worth in a business contract.
band_9_fix: Unless we 'quantify-the-value' of nature, it remains an 'economic-invisible'; by creating biodiversity-credits, we force the market to recognize that destroying a forest is a literal 'capital-loss', not just a moral sadness.

keyword: ielts speaking part 3: soil degradation and the foundation of civilization
problem_name: The dirt is always there and it will always grow food for us
band_5_example: Plants have been growing for a million years and they will keep growing forever as long as we have sun and water and some fertilizer from the shop.
band_9_fix: Soil is a 'non-renewable-biological-asset'; it takes centuries to form an inch of topsoil but only a decade of chemical-monoculture to destroy it. Civilization is structurally founded on 'agricultural-surplus', which is now under systemic-threat from erosion.

keyword: ielts writing task 2: the impact of micro-mobility on urban logistics
problem_name: Everyone should walk because e-scooters are for lazy people
band_5_example: I think people who use e-scooters are just lazy and they should walk or take a bike, because scooters block the sidewalk and they are bad for the old people.
band_9_fix: Micro-mobility is a 'logistical-lever'; it solves the 'last-mile-inefficiency' that often makes public-transport slower than a private car. By bridging this gap, we can actually increase the total-usage of high-capacity trains and buses.

keyword: ielts task 2: screen time and adolescent attention spans
problem_name: Kids these days can't focus on anything for more than five minutes
band_5_example: Young people are always looking at their phones and they have no patience anymore to read a whole book or listen to a long lecture from their teacher in school.
band_9_fix: The 'hyper-fragmentation' of digital media trains the brain for 'shallow-processing'; the constant switching between notifications prevents the individual from entering a 'flow-state', which is the only environment where complex, high-status logic can be produced.

keyword: ielts speaking part 3: influencer marketing and the erosion of expert authority
problem_name: I listen to influencers because they are cool and successful people
band_5_example: If someone has a million followers on Instagram, they must be smart and they must know which diet or which phone is the best for everyone to buy and use everyday.
band_9_fix: Influencer culture often replaces 'specialized-expertise' with 'perceived-status'; the audience accepts a recommendation based on the influencer's 'visual-leverage' rather than their actual diagnostic-capability or empirical-evidence.

keyword: ielts writing task 2: the 4-day work week and human capital retention
problem_name: Teachers will leave their jobs if they have to work four days only
band_5_example: If we change the work week, then we won't have enough people in hospitals or schools because everyone will want to be at home on Friday having a long weekend.
band_9_fix: A shorter week can act as a 'recruitment-multiplier'; in a competitive labor market, offering a superior 'time-sovereignty' protocol allows organizations to attract and retain the highest-tier talent without necessarily increasing the fiscal-salary.

keyword: ielts task 2: urban farming: the logistical bypass of nutrition
problem_name: Veggies from a roof aren't as good as veggies from a real farm
band_5_example: I think food grown in the city is not healthy because of the smoke and the cars, and it's better to get everything from a farmer in the country far away.
band_9_fix: Urban-farming utilizes 'controlled-environment-agriculture'; by growing food in a lab-like city environment, we eliminate the need for pesticides and the massive carbon-load of 'nutritional-logistics', providing a cleaner and more secure food source.

keyword: ielts speaking part 1: talking about using maps: digital vs mental
problem_name: I love my GPS because I never have to remember any street names ever again
band_5_example: I just put the address in my phone and I follow the blue line, and I don't care about the name of the road or the building because the phone knows everything for me.
band_9_fix: Externalizing our 'spatial-logic' to a device erodes our 'mental-cartography'; we move through the world as 'passive-conduits' rather than 'active-navigators', which can negatively impact our baseline navigational and orienting-intelligence.

keyword: ielts writing task 2: impact of globalization on food supply resilience
problem_name: It's great that I can eat an avocado in India whenever I want to
band_5_example: I love that I can go to the shop and buy food from any country in the world every single day, it makes me feel like a global citizen and I'm very happy.
band_9_fix: Globalized caloric-sourcing creates 'just-in-time-fragility'; while it provides variety, it means that a single geopolitical or transit-stoppage can trigger an immediate 'nutritional-deficit' in a nation that no longer produces its own basic staples.

keyword: ielts task 2: brand identity as tribal signaling in urban society
problem_name: I buy a Rolex because it is a very good and accurate watch for the time
band_5_example: I work hard and I want to have the best items, and a famous brand watch tells the time better than a cheap one from the market near my house.
band_9_fix: High-status branding is 'costly-signaling'; in a high-density, anonymous city, a brand acts as a 'visual-shorthand' for your resource-security and mate-value, allowing people to categorize you instantly without having to know your actual biography.

keyword: ielts speaking part 3: state-funded foundational research vs short-term profit
problem_name: Companies should do all the science because they are faster than the government
band_5_example: I think the government is slow and they waste money, and if a company wants to make a new drug or a new robot, they will work much harder to get the result and sell it.
band_9_fix: Market-driven research is bottlenecked by 'quarterly-incentives'; companies only fund what can be 'monetized-immediately'. True systemic-progress rely on state-funded 'blue-skies-research'—the kind of science where the big payoff might be 30 years away.

keyword: ielts writing task 2: local markets vs supermarket sterile efficiency
problem_name: Markets are dirty and it's better to shop in a clean grocery store with a card
band_5_example: I prefer the supermarket because it's clean and I don't have to talk to anyone and I can just pay and leave quickly with my plastic bags of food.
band_9_fix: Local-markets provide 'social-friction', which is essential for community-health; the interaction between buyer and seller creates a 'web-of-accountability' and localized-trust that the anonymous transaction of a supermarket lacks.

keyword: ielts speaking part 1: talking about your favorite clothes: decision fatigue
problem_name: I have 100 shirts so I can pick a different one every day and look cool
band_5_example: I love fashion and I spend half an hour every morning picking my outfit because it makes me feel professional and I want everyone at the office to see my style.
band_9_fix: I utilize a 'capsule-wardrobe' to eliminate 'decision-attrition'; by limiting my choices to a few high-quality, interchangeable items, I preserve my finite cognitive energy for my high-stakes professional output rather than morning-logistics.

keyword: ielts writing task 2: digital algorithms and political echo chambers
problem_name: My social media shows me things I like so I'm always happy to read the news
band_5_example: I love that my feed only has my favorite ideas and my favorite people, it makes the internet feel like a nice place where everyone agrees with me and we are all safe.
band_9_fix: Algorithmic curation creates 'ideological-incest'; when we are only exposed to data that confirms our existing biases, our 'critical-reasoning-muscles' atrophy, making us highly vulnerable to systemic misinformation and radicalization.

keyword: ielts task 2: high-speed rail vs aviation: regional transit ethics
problem_name: Planes are better because they are twice as fast as the fastest train
band_5_example: If I want to go to another city, I fly because it takes one hour and the train takes three, and I am a busy person so my time is more important than the environment.
band_9_fix: High-speed rail is 'logistically-superior' for regional hubs; once you account for airport-security and transit-to-terminal, a train is often faster and allows for continuous 'high-level-output' while traveling, unlike the fragmented experience of flying.

keyword: ielts speaking part 3: celebrities as narrative amplifiers in social causes
problem_name: Actors should just keep quiet and do their work in front of the camera
band_5_example: I hate it when a movie star starts talking about the earth or about politics; they are not experts and they are just doing it so people like them and follow them more.
band_9_fix: Celebrities are 'attention-multipliers'; while they lack 'surgical-expertise', their hyper-visibility can catapult a niche structural-issue into the 'mainstream-consciousness', forcing a political engagement that a thousand scientists could not achieve alone.

keyword: ielts writing task 2: remote work and urban de-centralization
problem_name: People only live in cities because they have to be in the office
band_5_example: The only reason we have high rent and crowded subways is because jobs are in the city center. If we work from home, everyone will move to the forest and mountains.
band_9_fix: Telecommuting allows for 'geographic-arbitrage'; however, the 'social-capital' and 'spontaneous-collaboration' of urban-hubs cannot be replicated on Zoom. Cities will pivot from 'transit-centers' to 'lifestyle-hubs' rather than disappearing.

keyword: ielts task 2: impact of genetic engineering on healthcare
problem_name: We shouldn't mess with DNA because it's not natural
band_5_example: I think it is dangerous to change the genes of a baby or a plant because we don't know what will happen and it is like playing God in a laboratory.
band_9_fix: CRISPR technology offers a 'surgical-correction' for hereditary pathologies; it is the only mechanism for eliminating systemic-suffering at the source, rather than just managing symptoms through lifetime-medication.

keyword: ielts speaking part 3: the psychology of panic buying during a crisis
problem_name: People are just greedy and they want to have more bread than their neighbors
band_5_example: When there is a war or a virus, people go to the shop and buy everything because they are selfish and they don't want other families to have any food.
band_9_fix: Panic-buying is a 'rational-survival-response' to 'informational-scarcity'; when the state fails to provide a clear logistical-guarantee, the individual reverts to a 'hyper-local-hoarding-protocol' to ensure their own group's viability.

keyword: ielts writing task 2: mandatory voting and democratic health
problem_name: Everyone should be forced to vote or they should pay a fine
band_5_example: In many countries, only 50% of people vote, which is bad. The government should make it a law that you must pick a leader or you get a punishment from the police.
band_9_fix: Compulsory voting creates a 'legitimacy-floor'; it prevents political candidates from only targeting 'extreme-demographics' and forces a shift towards 'moderate-consensus-building' to appeal to the entire population.

keyword: ielts task 2: the ethics of surveillance in the workplace
problem_name: Bosses should see every click of the mouse to make sure work is done
band_5_example: If an employer pays you for eight hours, they have the right to put a camera on your desk and track your keyboard to ensure you are not watching YouTube during the day.
band_9_fix: Micro-surveillance destroys 'organizational-trust'; it prioritizes 'mechanical-presence' over 'cognitive-output', incentivizing workers to perform a 'theatre-of-busyness' rather than engaging in high-status problem-solving.

keyword: ielts speaking part 3: the role of architecture in mental health
problem_name: Buildings are just boxes for people to live and work in
band_5_example: I don't care what a building looks like as long as it has a roof and it is warm inside and the rent is not too high for my family.
band_9_fix: Poorly designed high-density housing acts as a 'sensory-deprivation-chamber'; without access to natural light and 'biophilic-elements' (greenery), the architectural environment can trigger chronic cortisol-elevation and systemic-depression.

keyword: ielts writing task 2: the impact of artificial intelligence on creative arts
problem_name: Robots can't paint because they have no soul or feelings
band_5_example: A computer can only follow instructions, so it can never make a beautiful song or a painting that makes a human cry because it doesn't know what love is.
band_9_fix: Generative-AI is a 'style-distiller'; while it lacks 'natal-intent', it can recombine the entire history of human aesthetic-patterns with a 'computational-efficiency' that far exceeds individual human capability, effectively democratizing high-tier creative-production.

keyword: ielts task 2: ocean plastic and the global supply chain
problem_name: We just need to stop using straws and the sea will be clean
band_5_example: The reason the turtles are dying is because people use too many plastic straws in their drinks, and if we use paper ones, the problem is solved immediately.
band_9_fix: Individual consumption is a 'distraction-variable'; the vast majority of ocean-plastic comes from 'industrial-leakage' and 'abandoned-fishing-gear'. True remediation requires a 'producer-liability-model' at the global logistical level.

keyword: ielts speaking part 2: describe a piece of technology you enjoy using
problem_name: I love my air fryer because it makes healthy fries very fast
band_5_example: My favorite machine in the kitchen is the air fryer because I can put a potato inside and in 10 minutes I have food that is not oily and tastes great for dinner.
band_9_fix: I am particularly impressed by the 'thermodynamic-efficiency' of the device; it utilizes 'high-velocity-convection' to replicate the results of deep-frying with 90% less lipid-input, effectively bridging the 'convenience-nutrition-gap'.

keyword: ielts writing task 2: space tourism and environmental impact
problem_name: Rich people should spend their money on Earth instead of rockets
band_5_example: It is very unfair that a billionaire spends a hundred million dollars to fly into space for 10 minutes while there are homeless people in the same city.
band_9_fix: Space-tourism is the 'funding-engine' for orbital-infrastructure; without the influx of private-capital from high-net-worth individuals, we cannot develop the reusable-rockets necessary for 'off-world-industrialization'—the only way to remove heavy-polluting industries from Earth's atmosphere.

keyword: ielts task 2: the psychology of influencer culture: the 'idealized-self'
problem_name: Influencers are just people who take nice photos on holiday
band_5_example: I like looking at Instagram because the people are beautiful and they have a perfect life, and it makes me want to work harder so I can be like them in the future.
band_9_fix: Influencers provide a 'curated-mirage'; by projecting an 'idealized-self' through digital filters, they create a 'deficit-logic' in the audience, where the viewer feels structurally inadequate unless they consume the products being endorsed.

keyword: ielts speaking part 3: the ethics of targeted advertising to children
problem_name: Kids need to learn about new toys from the TV or they'll be sad
band_5_example: It's fine for children to see ads for cereal and toys because they need to tell their parents what they want for their birthday, and it's good for the business.
band_9_fix: Advertising to the pediatric demographic is 'neuro-exploitation'; it bypasses the underdeveloped executive-function of the child to instill 'brand-loyalty' and 'consumer-desire' before the individual has the logical-defenses to resist.

keyword: ielts writing task 2: the impact of noise pollution on urban wildlife
problem_name: Birds are fine in the city because they find food in the trash
band_5_example: I don't think animals care about the noise of cars and planes because they have lived in London and New York for a long time and they are happy as long as they eat.
band_9_fix: Auditory-saturation acts as a 'communication-bottleneck' for urban-fauna; many species have to 'frequency-shift' their mating-calls or abandon the city entirely because the 'technophony' (man-made noise) masks their survival-signals.

keyword: ielts task 2: the role of the public library in the digital age
problem_name: Libraries are useless because we have Wikipedia and Google now
band_5_example: Why does the government spend money on a big building with old paper books when every child has a phone in their pocket that has more information than a million libraries?
band_9_fix: Libraries have pivoted from 'book-repositories' to 'last-mile-digital-hubs'; they provide the 'physical-commons' and reliable-internet-access for low-income populations who would otherwise be structurally excluded from the digital-economy.

keyword: ielts speaking part 3: the geopolitics of the Arctic: ice melt and trade
problem_name: The ice melting is good because ships can get to Russia faster
band_5_example: I'm happy that the Arctic is melting because now boats can take a shortcut between the Atlantic and Pacific, which makes trade faster and things cheaper for everyone.
band_9_fix: The thawing of the Arctic creates a 'strategic-vacuum'; nations are now racing to claim the 'resource-rich-continental-shelf' that was previously inaccessible, potentially triggering a new 'gold-rush' that could destabilize global maritime-security.

keyword: ielts writing task 2: fast fashion and the 'disposable-culture'
problem_name: I buy cheap shirts because I like to look different every single week
band_5_example: It's great that I can buy a shirt for $5 and wear it once to a party and then throw it away, because it's so cheap that I don't care if it gets ruined or dirty.
band_9_fix: Fast-fashion is an 'environmental-debt-accelerator'; it relies on 'labor-arbitrage' in the global-South to produce 'sub-standard-garments' that are engineered to fail, forcing the consumer into a 'recursive-purchasing-loop' that generates a massive waste-load.

keyword: ielts task 2: the impact of high-speed internet on rural depopulation
problem_name: Farmers will stay on the farm if they can watch Netflix
band_5_example: The reason young people leave the village is because they are bored, so if you give them fast internet and a laptop, they will stay and help their parents grow rice.
band_9_fix: Broadband-connectivity is a 'utility-equalizer'; it allows for 'high-status-tele-work' to be performed from rural-locations, effectively removing the 'economic-gravity' of the city and allowing for a sustainable repopulation of the countryside.

keyword: ielts speaking part 2: describe a time you felt very busy
problem_name: I had many exams in one week and I had to study for 10 hours a day
band_5_example: Last month was very stressful for me because I had five different tests for my university degree and I didn't have time to see my friends or have a proper lunch.
band_9_fix: I was operating at 'peak-cognitive-capacity'; I had to utilize 'time-blocking' and 'prioritization-filters' to navigate a high-demand period where my bandwidth was the primary bottleneck to my success.

keyword: ielts writing task 2: the ethics of zoos: conservation vs entertainment
problem_name: Animals are happy in zoos because they get free food and no lions eat them
band_5_example: If you are a zebra in a zoo, you don't have to worry about a leopard killing you and you have a doctor who gives you medicine, so a zoo is like a luxury hotel for them.
band_9_fix: Zoological institutions are 'biological-banks'; while the 'captivity-deficit' for large mammals is undeniable, these facilities preserve the 'genetic-blueprints' of species that are currently undergoing 'habitat-extinction' in the wild.

keyword: ielts task 2: the role of traditional local markets in community health
problem_name: Markets are dirty and it's much better to go to the supermarket
band_5_example: I don't like the local market because it smells like fish and it's too hot, and in the supermarket, I have air-conditioning and a plastic bag for my groceries.
band_9_fix: Traditional markets provide 'social-friction', which is the essential 'connective-tissue' of a community. The transactional-anonymity of the supermarket erodes the 'neighborhood-trust' that local markets have sustained for centuries.

keyword: ielts speaking part 3: the impact of noise pollution on productivity
problem_name: I can't work when my neighbor is playing loud music
band_5_example: If someone is making noise outside my window, I can't read my book or do my homework because I'm distracted and I get angry and I want to shout at them.
band_9_fix: Noise is a 'cognitive-interrupter'; sustained 'ambient-clutter' prevents the brain from entering 'theta-state'—the high-focus zone necessary for 'deep-work'. Urban-design must prioritize 'auditory-privacy' to maintain the mental-health of the workforce.

keyword: ielts writing task 2: the impact of social media on friendship quality
problem_name: I have 5,000 friends on Facebook so I am a very popular and happy person
band_5_example: It's great that I can talk to thousands of people at the same time and see their vacation photos; it means I'm never lonely and I have many friends all around the world.
band_9_fix: Digital-connectivity has 'devalued-friendship-capital'; by replacing 'high-investment-physical-interaction' with 'low-effort-digital-feedback' (likes/comments), we have created a society that is 'hyper-connected' but 'micro-lonely'.

keyword: ielts task 2: the impact of cheap international flights on local culture
problem_name: Everyone goes to the same places and the world is boring now
band_5_example: Every city I visit looks exactly like my hometown with the same coffee shops and the same clothes stores, so there is no reason to travel anymore to see the world.
band_9_fix: Cheap-aviation has triggered 'cultural-globalization-attrition'; the influx of 'mass-tourism' forces local-economies to pivot towards a 'standardized-aesthetic' that caters to the visitor's comfort, effectively destroying the 'unique-otherness' that made the destination valuable.

keyword: ielts speaking part 3: the role of international organizations in global peace
problem_name: The UN is just a place for men in suits to talk and do nothing
band_5_example: I think international groups are a waste of money because they can't stop wars and they can't stop people from being poor; they just have meetings and dinner together.
band_9_fix: Supranational-governance provides a 'diplomatic-back-channel'; while they lack 'enforcement-sovereignty', they create the 'normative-frameworks' and 'communication-protocols' that prevent localized frictions from escalating into 'global-cascades' of conflict.

keyword: ielts writing task 2: the ethics of high salaries for celebrities vs teachers
problem_name: It's fair that a movie star gets more because everyone knows their name
band_5_example: If a hundred million people pay to see a film, the actor should get a lot of money; a teacher only has 30 students, so they shouldn't expect to be rich like a celebrity.
band_9_fix: Market-compensation is a 'scalability-variable'; an actor's output is 'near-zero-marginal-cost' and can be distributed globally, while a teacher's value-add is 'labor-intensive' and localized. The 'moral-wage-gap' is a result of this 'economic-reach-asymmetry'.

keyword: ielts task 2: the impact of online shopping on traffic congestion in cities
problem_name: Delivery vans are making the traffic move very slowly every day
band_5_example: I hate that there are so many Amazon and FedEx trucks on the street now; they block the road and they make a lot of noise and smoke, which makes me late for my work.
band_9_fix: The shift to e-commerce has relocated 'consumer-traffic' from private-cars to 'commercial-fleets'. This created a 'logistical-bottleneck' in city-centers that weren't designed for 'high-frequency-last-mile-delivery', necessitating a shift to 'autonomous-micro-hubs'.

keyword: ielts speaking part 1: talking about your favorite weather: productivity
problem_name: I like it when it's hot and sunny because I can go to the swimming pool
band_5_example: The best weather is 30 degrees Celsius with no clouds so I can wear a t-shirt and go to the beach with my friends and eat ice cream under the sun.
band_9_fix: I find that 'moderate-ambient-cooling' (around 20°C) significantly enhances my 'cognitive-throughput'; extreme heat or cold creates a 'physiological-distraction' that diverts bandwidth away from my professional focus.

keyword: ielts writing task 2: the importance of traditional storytelling for children
problem_name: Kids should just watch Netflix because the animation is better than a book
band_5_example: Why should a parent tell a story to a child when they can just put on a movie that has famous actors and amazing colors and music? It's more fun for the kid.
band_9_fix: Oral or written storytelling requires 'active-mental-rendering'; the child must build the 'perceptual-images' in their own mind, which develops the 'imaginative-muscles' that passive-video-consumption effectively allows to atrophy.

keyword: ielts task 2: the impact of urbanization on local food security
problem_name: We need more farms in the city so we don't starve if the trucks stop
band_5_example: If there is a war or a strike and the food trucks don't come to the city, we have nothing to eat. We should grow corn and potatoes in the public parks of the town.
band_9_fix: Metropolitan-areas are 'nutritively-vulnerable'; however, converting parks into low-yield-farms is a 'mis-allocation-of-space'. True security lies in 'vertical-hydroponic-integration'—using the city's 'vertical-surfaces' to produce high-density calories.

keyword: ielts speaking part 3: the role of museums in cultural identity
problem_name: Museums are just for keeping old things clean and safe from the rain
band_5_example: I think it's good that we have museums so that if I want to see a painting or a statue from 200 years ago, I can go to a big building and it will be there for me.
band_9_fix: Museums are 'civilizational-anchors'; they provide the 'physical-narrative' of a society's evolution. In a world of 'fleeting-digital-trends', these institutions maintain the 'temporal-continuity' that is essential for a stable collective identity.

keyword: ielts writing task 2: the ethics of surveillance technology in public spaces
problem_name: More cameras mean fewer criminals on the street so I feel safe
band_5_example: I think the police should have a eye on every corner of the city because if a bad person wants to steal my bag, they will see it and catch them quickly.
band_9_fix: Public-surveillance is a 'security-privacy-trade-off'; while it acts as a 'deterrent-mechanism' for petty-crime, it also creates a 'chilling-effect' on social-behavior, where the population begins to 'self-censor' their movements and associations for fear of being tracked.

keyword: ielts task 2: the importance of learning hand-writing in the computer age
problem_name: Kids don't need to write with a pen because they have a keyboard
band_5_example: Why do we spend hours teaching a child how to draw a letter 'A' on paper when they will work on a laptop for the next 50 years of their life? It's a waste of time.
band_9_fix: Hand-writing is a 'neurological-calibration'; the specific 'motor-memory' required to form letters connects different parts of the brain (the 'Haptic-Loop') that typing on a flat screen simply doesn't activate, improving memory and literacy.

keyword: ielts speaking part 2: describe a website you visit frequently
problem_name: I go to YouTube to watch funny videos when I have nothing better to do
band_5_example: My favorite site is YouTube because I can see people playing games or making jokes and it makes me laugh, and I can spend three hours there every single night.
band_9_fix: I utilize a 'curated-learning-aggregator'; I have programmed my algorithms to only surface data in the fields of 'game-theory' and 'macro-economics', effectively turning my leisure-time into an 'automated-skill-acquisition-window'.

keyword: ielts writing task 2: the impact of light pollution on human sleep cycles
problem_name: It's fine that the city is bright at night because it looks beautiful
band_5_example: I love the neon lights of Tokyo and New York at midnight because it feels like the city is alive and you can see everything like it's day, so it's safer to walk.
band_9_fix: Artificial-light-at-night (ALAN) is a 'circadian-disruptor'; it suppresses 'melatonin-production', which is the essential 'neurological-cleanup-agent'. This created a 'sleep-deficit-crisis' that is structurally linked to heart-disease and cognitive-decay in urban-populations.

keyword: ielts task 2: the ethics of performance-enhancing drugs in sports
problem_name: If a pill makes you faster, you should take it to win the game
band_5_example: Professional sports is about being the best in the world, so if a doctor can give you a medicine that makes your muscles bigger, you should be allowed to use it.
band_9_fix: PEDs create a 'biological-arms-race'; if we allow them, we effectively force every athlete to 'risk-their-long-term-health' just to remain competitive, destroying the 'natural-human-merit-standard' that makes sports ethically valuable.

keyword: ielts speaking part 3: the role of family in modern career success
problem_name: You can't get a good job if you don't have a rich father to help you
band_5_example: In my country, the most important thing is who you know and who your family is; if you are poor, it doesn't matter how hard you work, you will never be the boss.
band_9_fix: While 'nepotistic-networks' provide an 'entry-lever', modern high-status roles are increasingly 'algorithmically-vetted'. True career-longevity now depends on 'un-falsifiable-technical-excellence'—skills that family-connections cannot simulate in a competitive market.

keyword: ielts writing task 2: the impact of video games on social skills
problem_name: Gamers are lonely people who have no real friends in the world
band_5_example: If you play games for 5 hours a day, you don't know how to talk to a real person in the street, and you become a quiet and sad person who stays in your room.
band_9_fix: MMORPGs (Massive Online Games) are 'social-laboratories'; they require 'cross-border-collaboration' and 'complex-hierarchy-management' to achieve goals. For many, these digital-arenas provide a 'high-tier-leadership-training' that traditional school environments lack.

keyword: ielts task 2: the impact of sugar taxes on public health results
problem_name: Making soda expensive won't make people thin and healthy
band_5_example: If a bottle of Cola costs one dollar more, I will still buy it because I like it. The government should leave my money alone and let me drink what I want.
band_9_fix: Sugar-taxes are a 'friction-mechanism'; while they don't 'force' behavior-change, they shift the 'default-economic-choice'. Over a 10-year period, this 'nudging' can significantly reduce the 'metabolic-load' on national-healthcare-systems.

keyword: ielts speaking part 1: talking about using social media: networking
problem_name: I use Facebook to keep in touch with my cousins who live in other cities
band_5_example: I love that I can see the baby photos of my family members even if they are far away, and it makes me feel like we are still together in one house.
band_9_fix: I view social media as a 'professional-visibility-multiplier'; I utilize it to 'broadcast-my-surgical-logic-standard' to a global audience, effectively bypassing traditional 'gatekeepers' and building my own 'sovereign-authority-hub'.

keyword: ielts writing task 2: the impact of plastic packaging on global trade
problem_name: We should only buy food that isn't in a box or a plastic bag
band_5_example: The reason there is so much trash in the world is because every apple and every bread is in a plastic box. We should go back to the old way where everything was loose.
band_9_fix: Plastic-packaging is a 'shelf-life-logistics-fix'; while it's an environmental 'debt', it prevents 'massive-food-spoilage' during trans-continental transit. The fix is not 'no-packaging', but 'bio-degradable-polymers' that match the 'durability-utility' of plastic.

keyword: ielts task 2: the role of the father in modern parenting: logic vs emotion
problem_name: Mothers are for love and fathers are just for paying the bills
band_5_example: I think a mom's job is to stay home and hug the kids, and a dad's job is to work in an office for 12 hours so the family can have a nice house and a car.
band_9_fix: The 'paternal-variable' is essential for 'risk-calibration' and 'logical-boundaries'; modern child-development research shows that active fatherhood provides a 'distinct-cognitive-logic' that complements the 'nurturing-logic' of the mother, creating a balanced psyche.

keyword: ielts speaking part 3: the impact of space exploration on national pride
problem_name: It's great to see our flag on the moon because it makes us winners
band_5_example: I was so happy when my country sent a rocket to space because it means we are better than the other nations and our engineers are the smartest in the world.
band_9_fix: Space-achievements act as 'soft-power-demonstrators'; they signal to the world that a nation possesses the 'technological-precision' and 'capital-stability' necessary for the hardest possible tasks, attracting 'high-tier-foreign-investment' and 'top-level-talent'.

keyword: ielts writing task 2: the ethics of genetic privacy in the age of big data
problem_name: DNA companies should be allowed to sell your data to help science
band_5_example: If I send my spit to a company to see where my family came from, they should give that info to big pharma so they can make new pills to cure cancer for everyone.
band_9_fix: Genetic-data is your 'natal-source-code'; once it's 'externalized-to-a-corporate-server', it is potentially a 'permanent-blackmail-asset'. Insurance-companies could use your 'pathological-predispositions' to structurally exclude you from 'affordable-coverage' before you even get sick.

keyword: ielts task 2: the impact of artificial intelligence on human loneliness
problem_name: Robots will be our best friends because they never argue or fight
band_5_example: In 50 years, everyone will have a robot at home who listens to them and says nice things, and we will never be sad or lonely ever again in our life.
band_9_fix: AI-companions are a 'relational-hallucination'; they provide the 'surface-mimicry' of empathy without the 'reciprocal-cost' of a real human. This can create a 'social-atrophy', where people forget how to navigate the 'friction' and 'unpredictability' of real-world relationships.

keyword: ielts speaking part 3: the geopolitics of water security in the 21st century
problem_name: There is plenty of water in the ocean so we will never run out of it
band_5_example: I don't think water is a problem because we have rain and we have the big blue sea, so if we are thirsty, we just take the salt out and drink as much as we want.
band_9_fix: Potable-water is the 'foundational-commodity'; while desalination is a 'technical-bypass', it is incredibly 'energy-intensive'. Nations that share 'trans-boundary-river-systems' are currently entering a 'strategic-bottleneck' that could trigger the first 'resource-wars' of the century.

keyword: ielts writing task 2: the impact of short-form video on student grades
problem_name: TikTok is great for learning because the videos are short and easy
band_5_example: I love learning English on TikTok because the teacher only talks for 30 seconds and it's much more fun than reading a big boring book in a school library.
band_9_fix: Short-form-media creates an 'attentional-fragmentation'; by 'gaming-the-dopamine-loop', it trains the developing brain to expect a 'reward-spike' every minute, making the 'delayed-gratification' required for 'long-form-academic-success' logistically impossible for the student.

keyword: ielts task 2: the impact of meat-grown labs on the farming industry
problem_name: Lab-meat is gross and we should keep killing animals in the old way
band_5_example: I will never eat a burger made in a science lab by a man in a white coat; I want real meat from a real cow that lived in a real green field with grass.
band_9_fix: Cultured-protein is a 'resource-decoupling'; it allows for 'nutritional-production' without the 'carbon-overhead' and 'ethical-cost' of livestock. The primary hurdle is not 'biological-cleanliness', but 'cultural-inertia'—the human tendency to reject technical-superiority for 'nostalgic-comfort'.

keyword: ielts speaking part 3: the ethics of celebrity brand endorsements
problem_name: I buy a phone or a watch because a famous football player wears it
band_5_example: If a movie star says a perfume is good, it must be high-status and I want to smell like them so I will spend $100 to buy the same bottle in the shop today.
band_9_fix: Celebrity-endorsement is a 'status-contagion-tactic'; it relies on the 'primitive-shorthand' that if a 'top-tier-individual' uses an item, that item must be a 'fitness-indicator'. This bypasses 'logical-utility-analysis', forcing a 'mimetic-purchase' that the consumer doesn't actually need.

keyword: ielts writing task 2: the impact of universal basic income on entrepreneurship
problem_name: People will stay home and sleep if they get free money from the state
band_5_example: If the government gives everyone enough money for food and rent, nobody will start a business because they are comfortable being lazy and doing zero work.
band_9_fix: UBI acts as a 'risk-cushion'; it allows 'low-capital-individuals' to attempt 'high-status-business-ventures' without the fear of 'foundational-destitution'. This can trigger an 'innovation-blossom', as survival-friction is removed from the creative-equation.

keyword: ielts task 2: the impact of vertical cities on urban biodiversity
problem_name: We should only have small houses so the birds have more trees
band_5_example: If we build 100-story towers, we are killing nature because the sky belongs to the birds and the ground belongs to the insects and we should move away.
band_9_fix: Vertical-density is an 'environmental-preservation-lever'; by concentrating 10,000 people on one city-block, we prevent the 'horizontal-sprawl' that would have destroyed 500 acres of 'pristine-wilderness'. Density is the only way to save the 'global-green-lung'.

keyword: ielts speaking part 3: the role of art in political revolution
problem_name: Paintings and songs can't change the laws of a country
band_5_example: If people are unhappy with the government, they should go to the street and fight, not sit in a room and paint a picture or write a poem that nobody reads.
band_9_fix: Art is the 'narrative-vanguard'; before a law changes, the 'cultural-consensus' must change. Songs and visual-metaphors provide the 'emotional-fuel' and 'symbolic-unity' that allow a mass-population to coordinate their 'logical-dissent' against an outdated-system.

keyword: ielts writing task 2: the impact of the 4-day work week on childcare costs
problem_name: Parents will save money if they are home every Friday
band_5_example: If I don't go to work on one day of the week, I can take care of my own child and I don't have to pay a nanny or a nursery, which makes my family richer.
band_9_fix: A shorter work-week can reduce 'domestic-logistical-drain'; however, it may also lead to 'income-asymmetry' if wages are reduced. The real win is 'systemic-flexibility'—aligning 'economic-production' with 'human-biological-needs' rather than an arbitrary 19th-century-schedule.

keyword: ielts task 2: the ethics of state-monitored digital currencies
problem_name: Cash is better because the bank doesn't know what I buy for lunch
band_5_example: I want to keep paper money in my wallet because I don't want the government to see that I bought a chocolate bar or a new pair of shoes every Saturday afternoon.
band_9_fix: CBDCs (Central Bank Digital Currencies) provide 'total-fiscal-transparency'; while they eliminate 'tax-leakage' and 'illicit-flows', they also provide the state with a 'surgical-surveillance-tool' that can be used to 'de-platform' or 'fine-behavior' that is deemed socially-unwanted in near-real-time.

keyword: ielts speaking part 3: the impact of urbanization on noise sensitivity
problem_name: People in the city are annoyed by every little sound now
band_5_example: Because the town is so loud with buses and sirens, if a neighbor drops a spoon, people get angry because their brain is already tired of all the noise.
band_9_fix: 'Auditory-fatigue' lowers the 'tolerance-floor' for 'unexpected-stimulus'. In high-density environments, we must implement 'acoustic-zoning'—ensuring that every citizen has a 'sound-refuge'—to prevent a 'systemic-collapse-of-social-cohesion'.

keyword: ielts writing task 2: the impact of genetic engineering on biodiversity
problem_name: Scientists will make every cow the same and it's dangerous
band_5_example: If we only use the 'best' genes from a laboratory for all our food, then if one virus comes, it will kill every single plant and animal and we will all starve.
band_9_fix: 'Genetic-monoculture' is a 'systemic-fragility-risk'; while 'gene-editing' can optimize yield, we must maintain 'ancestral-seed-banks' and 'wild-population-diversity' to provide the 'evolutionary-redundancy' needed to survive a 'non-linear-pathogen-event'.

keyword: ielts task 2: the role of the public intellectual in the social media age
problem_name: Smart people should stay in the university and not use Twitter
band_5_example: If you are a professor with a PhD, you shouldn't be making 1-minute videos for 15-year-olds; it looks cheap and you should write a 500-page book instead.
band_9_fix: The 'ivory-tower-logic' is a 'failure-of-distribution'; if 'specialized-expertise' refuse to occupy 'high-leverage-digital-spaces', the 'informational-vacuum' is filled by 'low-tier-grifters'. Experts must become 'surgical-narrative-competitors' to save the public-discourse.

keyword: ielts speaking part 3: the impact of screen-time on face-to-face empathy
problem_name: We can't feel sorry for people if we only see them on a glass screen
band_5_example: If I see a sad video of a person crying, it's not the same as sitting next to them and holding their hand; my brain doesn't feel the same pain.
band_9_fix: Digital-interaction is 'empathy-lite'; the 'neurological-mirroring' required for deep-compassion relies on 'micro-expressions' and 'physical-proximity' that current video-compression technologies effectively 'filter-out', leading to a societal 'desensitization-effect'.

keyword: ielts writing task 2: the ethics of colonizing other planets and biospheres
problem_name: We should go to Mars and take what we want because it's dead
band_5_example: Mars has no trees and no water and no people, so we can just blow it up and dig for gold and build factories and it doesn't matter because nobody lives there.
band_9_fix: Mars is an 'exo-biological-laboratory'; until we verify the absence of 'extremophile-microbes', our presence is a 'biological-contamination-risk'. We must apply a 'planetary-protection-protocol' to ensure we don't 'accidently-extinguish' a unique-life-form before we even find it.

keyword: ielts task 2: the role of the public library in community 'third spaces'
problem_name: Libraries are just for books and nothing else happens there ever
band_5_example: If you want to meet people, you go to a cafe or a park; a library is a quiet place where you sit on your own and don't talk to anyone and read a paper book.
band_9_fix: Libraries are the 'last-remaining-non-commercial-commons'; as social-isolation increases, these buildings provide a 'cost-free-physical-anchor' where the 'socio-economic-status' of the individual is irrelevant to their right to exist in the space.

keyword: ielts speaking part 3: the impact of noise pollution on childhood learning
problem_name: Children are fine with TV noise in the background while they study
band_5_example: I think kids can learn to read and do math even if the window is open and there are cars going beep-beep outside; they just have to focus harder.
band_9_fix: 'Chronic-auditory-masking' in schools—typically from nearby airports or highways—has been shown to 'bottleneck-literacy-acquisition'. The brain's 'processing-power' is diverted to 'filter-out-the-noise', leaving less bandwidth for 'logical-encoding' of the lesson.

keyword: ielts writing task 2: the ethics of high-speed rail vs airline subsidies
problem_name: The government should make flying cheap so we can all go on holiday
band_5_example: I want the government to give money to the airlines so I can fly to Bali for $50; if they don't, then only rich people can see the world and that's not fair.
band_9_fix: Aviation-subsidies are an 'ecological-bailout'; by artificially lowering the price of 'high-carbon-transit', we structurally disincentivize the development of 'electric-high-speed-rail', which is the only 'geographically-logical' solution for regional transit-efficiency.

keyword: ielts task 2: the impact of artificial intelligence on the concept of 'truth'
problem_name: AI photos are great because they make everyone look like a model
band_5_example: I love using an app that makes me look younger and thinner; it's just for fun and it doesn't matter if it's not 'real' because it makes me feel good.
band_9_fix: AI-driven 'deep-fakes' and 'hallucinations' create an 'epistemic-collapse'; when 'visual-proof' can be 'algorithmically-synthesized' by anyone, the cost of 'verifying-reality' increases exponentially, moving society towards a state of 'total-distrust' in all distributed-media.

keyword: ielts speaking part 3: the geopolitics of semiconductor manufacturing
problem_name: A chip is just a small piece of dark plastic made in a big clean room
band_5_example: I think making a computer chip is easy if you have the machine, and we should just build a factory in every country so we never run out of laptops again.
band_9_fix: Semiconductor-fabrication is the 'ultimate-precision-bottleneck'; the world's most advanced chips are produced by a 'single-monopoly-entity' utilizing 'extreme-ultraviolet-lithography'. This 'geographical-concentration' is the primary 'strategic-choke-point' of the modern global-economy.

keyword: ielts writing task 2: the impact of universal secondary education on labor markets
problem_name: Everyone should have a degree even if they want to be a plumber
band_5_example: If you don't know about Shakespeare and the French Revolution, you are a primitive person; school should be for everyone until they are 21 years old.
band_9_fix: 'Mandatory-academic-prolongation' often leads to 'vocational-stagnation'; by forcing 'mechanically-gifted' individuals into 'abstract-academic-loops', we create a 'service-deficit' where there are too many 'generic-graduates' and zero 'expert-technicians' to maintain the physical-world.

keyword: ielts task 2: the ethics of targeted advertising on political elections
problem_name: Politicians should be allowed to send me news that I will like to hear
band_5_example: If a candidate knows I care about the environment, they should send me a video on Facebook about trees; it helps me decide who to vote for in my city.
band_9_fix: Political 'micro-targeting' utilizes 'data-asymmetry' to manipulate the voter's 'natal-fears'; by sending different 'mutually-exclusive-promises' to different groups, a candidate can bypass 'public-scrutiny' and avoid the 'logical-consistency' required for a healthy democracy.

keyword: ielts speaking part 3: the role of architecture in social stratification
problem_name: A building is just a building and it doesn't matter if it's fancy or not
band_5_example: I think it is fine to have big glass towers for rich people and small concrete blocks for poor people; it's just a matter of how much money you have in the bank.
band_9_fix: 'Hostile-architecture' and 'segregated-urban-design' reinforce 'class-boundaries'; when high-status-spaces are 'physically-walled-off' from the rest of the city, we destroy the 'accidental-empathy' that occurs when different socio-economic groups share the same 'visual-and-spatial-commons'.

keyword: ielts writing task 2: the impact of micro-mobility on urban noise levels
problem_name: E-scooters are quiet so they are dangerous because you can't hear them
band_5_example: The problem with electric bikes is that they don't make any sound, and if I am walking on the street, they might hit me because I didn't know they were behind me.
band_9_fix: The transition to 'silent-electric-fleets' represents a 'radical-auditory-reclamation'; while it requires 'acoustic-signatures' for safety, it eliminates the 'chronic-combustion-shriek' that previously defined urban-life, lowering the 'societal-stress-baseline'.

keyword: ielts task 2: the ethics of state-monitored digital currencies and financial freedom
problem_name: The government should see my bank account so they can catch tax cheats
band_5_example: I have nothing to hide, and if the police want to know how much I spend on coffee every month to make sure I'm a good citizen, they can look at my phone.
band_9_fix: Digital-fiat-currency (CBDC) allows for the 'programmability-of-money'; the state could structurally 'expire' your savings to force 'economic-stimulus' or block your 'purchasing-power' if you travel to a 'restricted-zone', effectively eliminating the concept of 'personal-fiscal-sovereignty'.

keyword: ielts speaking part 3: the geopolitics of rare earth metals: the green-energy trap
problem_name: Windmills and solar panels are made of free air and sun and nothing else
band_5_example: If you want to save the planet, you just build a big solar farm; you don't need any oil or coal, and the energy comes for zero dollars from the sky.
band_9_fix: The 'green-transition' is structurally dependent on 'rare-earth-logistics'; building a single EV battery requires more mining and 'mineral-processing' than a hundred internal-combustion engines. We are trading 'oil-dependence' for a new 'mineral-dependence' on highly-concentrated-geographies.

keyword: ielts writing task 2: the impact of 24/7 news cycles on human psychology
problem_name: It's great to know what is happening in the world every single minute
band_5_example: I love that I can wake up at 3 AM and see a live video of a plane crash or a war; it makes me feel informed and ready for anything that happens in my life.
band_9_fix: The 'constant-threat-stream' of 24/7 news mimics an 'evolutionary-emergency'; it keeps the 'amygdala' in a state of 'chronic-arousal', destroying 'long-term-strategic-logic' and replacing it with a 'reactive-panic-mode' that makes the population highly easy to manipulate.

keyword: ielts task 2: the ethics of facial recognition technology and public anonymity
problem_name: If you're not a criminal, you should be happy to have your face in the computer
band_5_example: I think it's very convenient to pay for my bus or my lunch just by looking at a camera; it's fast and I don't need my wallet or my credit card anymore.
band_9_fix: Public-anonymity is the 'foundational-buffer' of a free-society; once 'facial-biometrics' are integrated into the 'urban-grid', every 'non-standard-movement' (like attending a protest) is permanently linked to your 'citizen-profile', effectively turning the city into a 'digital-prison' without bars.

keyword: ielts speaking part 3: the impact of screen-time on childhood obesity rates
problem_name: Kids are fat because they like burgers and they don't like to eat salad
band_5_example: I don't think tablets have anything to do with weight; children just eat too much chocolate and they need to learn to be more healthy in their food choices at home.
band_9_fix: Screen-time is a 'sedentary-displacement-variable'; modern apps are engineered to provide 'high-frequency-dopamine' with 'zero-physical-output'. This creates a 'metabolic-mismatch' where the child is mentally-aroused but physically-stagnant, accelerating the 'obesity-cascade' regardless of diet.

keyword: ielts writing task 2: the role of the public intellectual in the disinformation era
problem_name: Smart people should speak more if we want to stop fake news
band_5_example: If you are a scientist, you should make a YouTube channel and tell everyone the truth about the world, so they stop listening to crazy people on the internet.
band_9_fix: Truth is 'distribution-bottlenecked'; while a scientist 'carefully-calibrates' a logical-premise, a disinformation-actor can generate '100-emotional-lies' using AI. To survive, the expert must learn to utilize 'surgical-asymmetry-narratives'—data that is so 'visceral-and-true' that it cannot be easily-faked.

keyword: ielts task 2: the ethics of genetic engineering: the 'designer-baby' debate
problem_name: Every parent should be allowed to make their child the best model
band_5_example: If I can pay a doctor to make my son tall, smart, and have blue eyes, then he will have a better life and I'm a good parent for giving him those things.
band_9_fix: Genetic-enhancement creates a 'biological-wealth-gap'; if only the rich can 'edit-the-source-code' of their offspring, we effectively move from 'socio-economic-inequality' to 'permanent-evolutionary-class-stratification', where the poor children are 'structurally-obsolete' at a DNA-level.

keyword: ielts speaking part 3: the impact of artificial intelligence on the legal system
problem_name: Robots can't be judges because they don't have a heart and they are cold
band_5_example: A judge should be a human with 30 years of experience who can look at a person and feel if they are sorry or if they are lying about the crime.
band_9_fix: AI-driven 'predictive-sentencing' provides 'systemic-consistency'; however, it also 'bakes-in-historical-bias' from the data it was trained on. A robot-judge might be 'fair' according to the rules, but 'blind' to the 'human-nuance' that defines true justice.

keyword: ielts writing task 2: the impact of short-form video on 'deep-reading' skills
problem_name: I prefer 10-second videos because a book takes too long to finish
band_5_example: Books are old and they don't have sound or movement, so they are boring and they make my brain tired. I can learn everything I need from a 15-second clip.
band_9_fix: Deep-reading is the 'logical-operating-system' for complex-thought; short-form-media is a 'continuous-interrupt-command' that prevents the development of the 'linear-attention' required to understand 'causal-chains' or 'systemic-logic', effectively lowering the 'civilizational-IQ'.

keyword: ielts task 2: the ethics of high-speed rail vs autonomous-EV-fleets
problem_name: A self-driving car is better than a train because it takes me to my door
band_5_example: I want a robot car that I can sleep in while it drives me to my office; a train is bad because I have to walk to the station and wait for 10 minutes in the rain.
band_9_fix: Autonomous-EVs are a 'congestion-multiplier'; even if they are clean, they utilize 'massive-spatial-resources' for a single individual. High-speed-rail is a 'spatial-efficiency-lever'; it is the only way to move 1,000 people through a 'geographic-conduit' with near-zero-waste of space.

keyword: ielts speaking part 3: the geopolitics of outer-space mining: the new gold rush
problem_name: Space is for everyone so we should go there and take what we find
band_5_example: If there is an asteroid with a billion tons of gold, we should just bring it back here and everyone will be rich and there will be no more poverty.
band_9_fix: Space-resources create 'commodity-instability'; bringing back a billion tons of gold would collapse the global market for that metal instantly. True 'off-world-value' lies in 'in-situ-utilization'—using space minerals to build the 'orbital-economies' that will eventually support ten-billion people.

keyword: ielts writing task 2: the impact of noise pollution on property values
problem_name: If a street is loud, then the houses should be very cheap for the poor
band_5_example: I think it's normal that if you live next to a highway or a nightclub, your house is not worth much money; if you want a quiet life, you should work harder and buy a house in a forest.
band_9_fix: 'Auditory-inequality' is a 'latent-health-tax'; the noise-load is structurally concentrated in low-income zones, meaning that 'economic-destitution' is permanently linked to 'chronic-neurological-stress' (from interrupted sleep), creating a 'poverty-loop' that's difficult to exit.

keyword: ielts task 2: the role of the public intellectual in the attention economy
problem_name: If nobody clicks on your video, it's because you are boring and wrong
band_5_example: In the modern world, the most important thing is how many likes you have; if a scientist says something but nobody shares it, then what he said doesn't really matter.
band_9_fix: 'Attention' is independent of 'Truth'; in a market where 'outrage-logic' and 'fear-loops' generate the most profit, 'boring-systemic-truths' are structurally disadvantaged. The intellectual's primary task is to 'package-truth-with-surgical-aesthetic' to survive the 'algorithmic-filter'.

keyword: ielts writing task 2: the impact of digital currencies on national sovereignty
problem_name: The government will lose control if we all use Bitcoin for bread
band_5_example: If I pay with a digital coin that the bank didn't make, then the leaders of my country won't know how much money is in the street, and they will be very worried.
band_9_fix: Decentralized-finance (DeFi) represents a 'monetary-decoupling'; it removes the state's ability to utilize 'inflationary-logic' as a fiscal-tool, potentially leading to a more stable 'global-value-standard' that is immune to localized political mismanagement.

keyword: ielts task 2: the ethics of fast food marketing in the developing world
problem_name: Burgers are a sign of wealth in poor countries so everyone wants them
band_5_example: In Africa or Southeast Asia, going to a famous American fast food shop makes you feel like a high-status person, so even if you are poor, you save money to buy a meal.
band_9_fix: Aggressive-expansion into 'emerging-markets' exploits a 'prestige-bias'; by linking 'western-standard-of-living' with 'low-nutrient-caloric-intake', corporations are effectively exporting a 'metabolic-health-deficit' to populations with minimal medical-safety-nets.

keyword: ielts speaking part 3: the role of art in urban regeneration
problem_name: A painting on a wall can make a poor neighborhood rich again
band_5_example: If we put a beautiful mural on an old building, people will come with their cameras, and then someone will open a cafe, and the whole town becomes happy.
band_9_fix: 'Creative-placemaking' acts as an 'economic-stimulus-catalyst'; however, without 'anti-displacement-policies', it often triggers 'aesthetic-gentrification', where the original 'social-fabric' is priced-out to satisfy the 'visual-consumption' of a new high-income class.

keyword: ielts writing task 2: the impact of the 'right to repair' on electronics waste
problem_name: If my phone is broken, I should be able to fix it with a screwdriver
band_5_example: Companies shouldn't make me buy a new laptop just because the battery is old; I want the shop down the road to be able to open it and fix the parts for a few dollars.
band_9_fix: Legislating the 'right-to-repair' is a 'structural-defense' against 'planned-obsolescence'; it forces manufacturers to move from a 'disposable-unit-model' to a 'modular-lifecycle-model', significantly lowering the 'material-extraction-load' on the planet.

keyword: ielts task 2: the ethics of data-harvesting for 'personalized-education'
problem_name: The computer should know everything I get wrong so it can help me
band_5_example: I love an app that remembers my mistakes and gives me 100 new questions about them; it's the fastest way to learn English and it's better than a human teacher.
band_9_fix: 'Personalized-pedagogy' utilizes 'behavioral-loop-analysis'; while it optimizes for 'short-term-retention', it also creates a 'data-profile' of the student's 'cognitive-singularities' (weaknesses), which could be commercially exploited by 'labor-market-algorithms' in the future.

keyword: ielts speaking part 3: the impact of autonomous-transit on 'drive-thru' economies
problem_name: If I don't have to park, I will buy my coffee while I sleep in the car
band_5_example: Self-driving cars mean I can stay in my seat and the robot will go to the window of the shop and get my food, so I never have to walk into a building again.
